document.source_title: Rethinking Security Cooperation in the Age of Commercial Tech
document.summary: The article discusses the increasing importance of commercial technologies in enhancing military capabilities and the need for the U.S. Department of Defense to adapt its security cooperation strategies. It highlights the limitations of traditional military solutions and the urgency for allies to adopt commercial technologies to remain competitive against adversaries like China and Russia. The piece emphasizes the necessity for a shift in U.S. policy to prioritize commercial solutions in military partnerships.
key_takeaways: Commercial technologies enhance military efficiency and effectiveness.
The U.S. defense industrial base struggles to meet production demands.
Traditional military solutions may not be suitable for all allies.
Ukraine's use of commercial tech exemplifies rapid military innovation.
The U.S. must evolve its security cooperation strategy to include commercial solutions.
Congress has mandated the inclusion of commercial technology in foreign military sales.
document.why_it_matters: This research is significant as it addresses the evolving nature of warfare and the critical role of commercial technologies in modern military operations. It underscores the need for the U.S. to adapt its security cooperation strategies to maintain a competitive edge and support allies effectively in a rapidly changing global threat landscape.

claims.claim_text: Commercial technologies are critical for modern warfare.
claims.evidence_text: Commercial technologies are enabling the U.S. military and militaries around the world to operate with greater efficiency, speed, precision, and lethality.

claims.claim_text: The U.S. defense industrial base is struggling to meet production demands.
claims.evidence_text: There is roughly $250 billion in backlog in foreign military sales, due in no small part to bottlenecks in weapons system production.

claims.claim_text: Ukraine is rapidly developing its arms industry using commercial technologies.
claims.evidence_text: Ukraine is rapidly developing its global arms industry because of its ability to rapidly innovate and produce 'good enough' solutions.

entities.name: Ukraine
entities.description: A country that has been rapidly developing its arms industry using commercial technologies.
entities.evidence_text: Ukraine is rapidly developing its global arms industry because of its ability to rapidly innovate and produce 'good enough' solutions.

entities.name: Russia
entities.description: An adversary of the U.S. that has launched a full-scale invasion of Ukraine.
entities.evidence_text: Three years before Russia launched its full-scale invasion of Ukraine, President Volodymyr Zelensky launched an ambitious effort to build a 'state in a smartphone.'

entities.name: China
entities.description: An adversary of the U.S. that poses a significant threat in the global military landscape.
entities.evidence_text: The benefits of commercial capabilities can be particularly impactful given the defense industrial base’s struggles to produce at the speed and scale needed to outmatch China.

entities.name: Department of Defense
entities.description: The U.S. government department responsible for coordinating and supervising all agencies and functions of the government relating directly to national security and the military.
entities.evidence_text: The Department of Defense should evolve its approach to security cooperation and further encourage commercial solutions.

metrics.name: Foreign Military Sales Backlog
metrics.evidence_text: There is roughly $250 billion in backlog in foreign military sales.

metrics.name: Record Foreign Military Sales Agreement
metrics.evidence_text: The administration is now pursuing significant foreign military sales deals, such as a record $142 billion agreement with Saudi Arabia.

citations.title: Rethinking Security Cooperation in the Age of Commercial Tech

source_credibility.reason: The article is published on a reputable platform focused on military and security analysis.
